meta.title: Safety Plays
meta.description: Giving up maximum tricks to guarantee the contract

blocks.content:
## The Art of Giving Up

A **safety play** deliberately sacrifices the maximum possible number of tricks in a suit to guarantee enough tricks against bad breaks. It's insurance — you pay a small premium (a potential overtrick) to protect against a large loss (going down).

Safety plays are the hallmark of expert declarer play. They require you to think about what can go wrong before it does — and to give up a little to secure a lot.

## The Classic Position

```
Dummy:  x x x
You:    A K x x x
```

You need 4 tricks from this suit. The opponents have 5 cards.

### If the suit splits 3-2 (68% of the time):
Play the Ace, then the King. Both opponents follow. You've taken 5 tricks — the maximum.

### If the suit splits 4-1 (28% of the time):
If you play the Ace and King, one opponent shows out on the second round. Now the other opponent has 2 winners in the suit. You've lost control.

### The Safety Play:
Play the **Ace first** (both follow). Then play a **small card** toward dummy. If both opponents follow, you've already set up the suit. If one opponent shows out, the other has 3 remaining — but you still have the King and can manage the suit to take your 4 tricks.

By playing Ace, then small (instead of Ace-King), you guarantee 4 tricks against any 4-1 break, at the cost of sometimes winning only 4 tricks when you could have won 5.

## When You Need All the Tricks

Safety plays only make sense when you can afford to lose a trick. If you need **all 5 tricks** from AKxxx opposite xxx, there's no safety play — you must play for the 3-2 break and hope.

This is why safety plays are context-dependent: they depend on how many tricks you need from the suit, not just the holding.

## More Safety Play Positions

### Position 2: AKT9x opposite xxx

You need 4 tricks. Missing QJxxx.

If the suit is 3-2 with Q-J split: Ace-King catches both honors. Maximum tricks.

But if one opponent has QJxx: Ace-King only catches one honor. You lose 2 tricks.

**Safety play:** Cash the **Ace** (both follow with small cards). Then lead small toward the **T9**. If West plays the Q or J, win the King and finesse the T against the remaining honor. If West plays small, insert the **T** (or **9**). This guarantees 4 tricks against any 3-2 or 4-1 break where West has length.

### Position 3: AQxxx opposite Kxx

You need 5 tricks. Missing J-10-9-x-x.

**Normal play:** Cash AK, hoping the Jack drops in 2 rounds. Works when the suit is 3-2 with the J onside.

**Safety play for 4 tricks:** Cash the **King** first (from the short side). Then lead toward the **AQxxx**. If West shows out, you can finesse East's J through the AQ. If East shows out, West has Jxxx — but you've already cashed the King and can play Ace and concede one trick.

Cashing the honor from the short side first is a common safety play technique.

### Position 4: AJ9xx opposite Kxx

You need 4 tricks. Missing Q-10-x-x-x.

**Safety play:** Cash the **King** first. Then lead toward the AJ9. If West plays the Queen, win the Ace — the remaining cards are good. If West plays small, finesse the **9** (or **J**). This handles Q with West in any distribution. If East has Qxxx, you need the finesse — and starting with the King from the short side preserves your options.

## The Golden Rule of Safety Plays

> **Cash the honor in the shorter hand first, then lead toward the longer hand.**

This keeps your options open. If someone shows out, you can still finesse through the remaining honors.

## When to Use Safety Plays

### At IMPs: Almost Always

At IMPs, making your contract is paramount. An overtrick is worth 1 IMP. Going down costs 10+. Safety plays are automatic.

### At Matchpoints: Only When the Contract is Unusual or the Risk is High

At matchpoints, overtricks matter. If everyone is in 4♠, making 5 (with an overtrick) beats making 4. So at matchpoints, you might skip a safety play when:
- The contract is normal (everyone else is here too)
- The risk of a bad break is small
- The overtrick would be a big matchpoint gain

But even at matchpoints, use a safety play when:
- You're in a unusual contract (yours to make or go down)
- You're vulnerable (the cost of going down is higher)
- You're in slam (going down in slam is a disaster at any scoring)

## Thinking About Safety Plays

Before playing the first card of a suit, ask yourself:

1. **How many tricks do I need from this suit?**
2. **What's the worst break that could happen?**
3. **Is there a way to play the suit that handles that worst break?**
4. **What does the safety play cost me?** (Usually just an overtrick.)

If the cost is small and the protection is large, use the safety play.

blocks.title: Safety Play at IMPs
blocks.solution:
You're in 6♠ at IMPs. Trumps are:

```
Dummy:  ♠ A K 7 5 3
You:    ♠ Q 9 8 6 2
```

You have 10 trumps between you. The opponents have ♠JT4. You need to lose zero trump tricks. How do you play?

**The danger:** If spades are 3-0 (one opponent has JT4), playing AK might leave that opponent with a winner.

**The safety play:** Cash the ♠A first. If both follow — great, play the King next. If **West shows out** (East has JT4), lead toward your Q98 and finesse East's JT through your honors. If **East shows out** (West has JT4), lead from dummy toward your Q9 — you can finesse West.

By cashing just the Ace first (not AK), you discover the 3-0 break before it's too late. The cost? None, really — you're going to play the suit the same way if it's 2-1. You're just getting information before committing.

In slam at IMPs, this care is essential. Going down in 6♠ because of a careless trump play is a disaster — 13+ IMPs lost.

blocks.title: Choosing Between Maximum and Safety
blocks.solution:
You're declaring 4♥ at matchpoints. Your heart suit:

```
Dummy:  ♥ A K 6 3 2
You:    ♥ 8 7 5
```

You need 4 heart tricks to make your contract. The opponents have ♥QJT9. How do you play?

**For maximum tricks:** Play AK and hope the suit is 2-2 (40%) or someone has a singleton Q/J/T/9 that drops. If it's 3-1 with QJT onside, you lose 2 tricks.

**Safety play for 4 tricks:** Play the **Ace** (both follow). Then play a **small heart** from dummy, conceding a trick. The opponents win, but now — regardless of how the remaining cards split — you can come back and play the King, setting up the 5th card as a winner. You lose exactly 1 heart trick, guaranteeing 4.

**At matchpoints:** If most pairs are in 4♥, playing for the maximum (AK, hoping for 2-2 or a lucky drop) might be right — overtricks matter. If the suit is 2-2, you take 5 tricks and beat the field.

**At IMPs:** Play safe! Cash the Ace, then duck. You guarantee your contract. The overtrick is irrelevant.

This is the matchpoint vs IMP tension in its purest form.

reviewQuestions.question: What is a safety play?
options: Deliberately giving up maximum tricks to guarantee enough tricks against bad breaks
reviewQuestions.explanation: A safety play sacrifices potential overtricks to protect against bad suit splits, ensuring you make your required number of tricks.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's right! Safety plays are insurance — giving up a little to protect against a lot.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: A safety play gives up the chance for maximum tricks to guarantee enough tricks against bad breaks. It's declarer insurance.

reviewQuestions.question: When should you almost always use a safety play?
options: At matchpoints when you need overtricks
At IMPs when making the contract is paramount
reviewQuestions.explanation: At IMPs, the contract matters far more than overtricks. Safety plays gain little (1 IMP lost) but protect enormously (10+ IMPs saved).
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly! At IMPs, the math overwhelmingly favors safety. Overtricks are spare change.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: At IMPs, making your contract is everything. An overtrick gains ~1 IMP; going down costs 10+. Safety plays are automatic at IMPs.
